original_assessment.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Navigate to the Azure Portal.
2. In the left sidebar, select “Storage accounts.”
3. Click on the specific storage account you want to audit.
4. Under “Data storage,” select “Containers.”
5. For each container, click on the three dots (more options) next to the container name.
6. Select “Access policy.”
7. Review the access policies listed. If any SAS tokens have been created with HTTP as an allowed protocol, take note of them.
8. If needed, click on the three dots next to the access policy and select “Delete” to revoke the SAS.
9. If SAS tokens were not created with a stored access policy, go back to the storage account overview.
10. Under “Security + networking,” select “Access keys.”
11. Click “Rotate key” next to each key to regenerate the access keys, thereby revoking any SAS tokens created with those keys.
12. Confirm any actions and save changes as necessary.

**What to verify/validate:** Confirm that all SAS tokens allow HTTPS only and no HTTP allowed protocols are present.

**Expected secure configuration:** All SAS tokens should have "Allowed protocols" set to "HTTPS Only."

**How to confirm compliance:** Ensure that any SAS tokens allowing HTTP have been revoked and that new SAS tokens will only be generated with HTTPS as the allowed protocol moving forward.

review.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE
- The classification as Manual is partially correct. While manual verification is possible, this control can also be automated using Azure APIs, Azure CLI, or PowerShell. Azure provides capabilities to list and manage SAS tokens programmatically.
- Azure CLI and PowerShell can be used to check SAS token properties. For example, using `az storage container generate-sas` with the `--https-only` parameter can ensure HTTPS-only SAS tokens.
- The Azure Resource Manager (ARM) API and Azure SDKs can also be leveraged to automate the verification of SAS token protocols.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 6
- The manual steps are generally accurate but incomplete. They focus on stored access policies but do not address SAS tokens created directly without stored policies.
- The steps to rotate access keys are not directly related to ensuring HTTPS-only SAS tokens and may not be necessary unless revoking all tokens is required.
- The naming convention for the program is not applicable here as no specific program is mentioned.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- Include automated methods using Azure CLI or PowerShell to check for SAS tokens allowing HTTP. This will streamline the process and reduce human error.
- Clarify the distinction between stored access policies and ad-hoc SAS tokens, as both need to be checked.
- Provide specific CLI or PowerShell commands to list and verify SAS tokens, such as `az storage account show` or `Get-AzStorageAccount`.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM
- The assessment is mostly accurate but lacks completeness and automation considerations, which are critical for efficiency and scalability.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Include automated checks using Azure CLI or PowerShell to verify SAS tokens. Provide specific commands and scripts that can be used to audit SAS tokens programmatically.
- Re-classify: Consider reclassifying the audit approach to Automated, as Azure provides sufficient tools to automate this compliance check.

input_row.title: Ensure 'Allowed Protocols' for shared access signature (SAS) tokens is set to 'HTTPS Only'
input_row.description: Shared access signatures (SAS) can be used to grant limited access to Azure Storage resources. When generating a SAS, it is possible to specify the allowed protocols for a request made with the SAS. It is recommended to allow requests over HTTPS only.
input_row.rationale: If a SAS is passed over HTTP and intercepted, an attacker performing a man-in-the- middle attack can read the SAS. Then, they can use that SAS just as the intended user could have. This can potentially compromise sensitive data or allow for data corruption by the malicious user. Impact: SAS can pose security risks if they are not managed carefully.
input_row.audit: It is not possible to audit generated SAS.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal If SAS have been created to allow HTTP and were created with a stored access policy (SAP), the SAS can be revoked by deleting the SAP or updating the SAP expiration time to a time in the past: 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Data storage, click Containers. 4. Click the three dots next to a container. 5. Click Access policy. 6. Click the three dots next to an access policy. 7. Click Delete. 8. Click Save. 9. Repeat steps 1-8 as needed to revoke SAS created with SAP.  If SAS have been created to allow HTTP and were not created with a SAP, the SAS can be revoked by regenerating the storage account access keys: Note: Regenerating access keys can affect any applications or Azure services that are dependent on the storage account key. 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Security + networking, click Access keys. 4. Next to each key, click Rotate key. 5. Click Yes to confirm. 6. Repeat steps 1-5 as needed to revoke SAS. Default Value: When generating a SAS, the default selection for Allowed protocols is HTTPS only. References: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas-overview 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-keys- manage Additional Information: This recommendation is based on the recommendation Ensure 'Allowed Protocols' for shared access signature (SAS) tokens is set to 'HTTPS Only', from the Common Reference Recommendations > Secrets and Keys > Shared Access Signatures section.
input_row.impact: SAS can pose security risks if they are not managed carefully.
input_row.reference: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas-overview 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-keys- manage Additional Information: This recommendation is based on the recommendation Ensure 'Allowed Protocols' for shared access signature (SAS) tokens is set to 'HTTPS Only', from the Common Reference Recommendations > Secrets and Keys > Shared Access Signatures section.
